summarization.a: Leakage current density versus electric field for 10 nm Ta₂O₅ films grown at 250°C. PE-ALD yields approximately two orders of magnitude lower leakage (~8×10⁻⁷ A/cm²) compared to thermal ALD (~6×10⁻⁵ A/cm²) at 1 MV/cm.
summarization.b: Leakage current density versus electric field for 10 nm TiO₂ films grown at 200°C. PE-ALD again shows lower leakage (~7×10⁻⁴ A/cm²) than thermal ALD (~5×10⁻³ A/cm²) at 1 MV/cm, though the difference is smaller than for Ta₂O₅.

a.question: How do the leakage current densities of PE-ALD and thermal ALD Ta₂O₅ films compare at 1 MV/cm?
a.answer: PE-ALD Ta₂O₅ exhibits a leakage current density of approximately 8×10⁻⁷ A/cm² at 1 MV/cm, which is about two orders of magnitude lower than thermal ALD Ta₂O₅ at approximately 6×10⁻⁵ A/cm². This substantial improvement makes PE-ALD Ta₂O₅ from PDMAT competitive with or better than previously reported values for PE-ALD Ta₂O₅ from ethoxide precursors.

a.question: What factors might explain the lower leakage current in PE-ALD films compared to thermal ALD?
a.answer: Lower oxygen vacancy concentration, reduced carbon or silicon contamination, and smoother oxide/substrate interfaces observed by TEM.

a.question: Which oxide and deposition method combination shows the best electrical performance?
a.answer: PE-ALD Ta₂O₅, with the lowest leakage current density of approximately 8×10⁻⁷ A/cm² at 1 MV/cm.

b.question: Is the leakage current improvement from using PE-ALD greater for Ta₂O₅ or TiO₂?
b.answer: The improvement is greater for Ta₂O₅. PE-ALD reduces the leakage current by approximately two orders of magnitude for Ta₂O₅ (from ~6×10⁻⁵ to ~8×10⁻⁷ A/cm²), whereas for TiO₂ the reduction is less than one order of magnitude (from ~5×10⁻³ to ~7×10⁻⁴ A/cm²).
